This is not just for those already diagnosed with endo, this is for parents, teachers and classmates too.  Let's spread the gwarnes about Endometriosis. Liz Bruen was the first endometriosis nurse in Wales.  She has now moved to a private virtual space, so if you want to book a consultation with her, go to the link in our bio.

post.caption: Always ask for your surgical photos after a laparoscopy.  Always. Those images are your medical records and you are entitled to them, but most people don't know to ask.  If your results came back clear but something still doesn't feel right, a specialist can review those photos and often spot what was missed.  Endometriosis doesn't always look the way surgeons expect, and subtle lesions are easily overlooked without the right training.  Your photos could be the difference between years more of fighting, and finally getting answers.  Liz covers exactly this in her virtual clinic - link in bio to book.

post.caption: A 30-minute laparoscopy that found nothing doesn't mean nothing is there.  A diagnostic laparoscopy is only as good as the person performing it, and not every surgeon is trained to spot every form of endometriosis. Endometriosis nurse Liz breaks down exactly why that happens, and shares something really important that every single person should do after their surgery: ask for your photos. Those images could make all the difference if you ever need a second opinion. Watch before your next appointment.

Because nobody should have to suffer in silence or feel too embarrassed to ask for help. The lanyard is free and there are no eligibility requirements, you simply request one. If you think it could help you, head to hdsunflower.com/uk to find out more.

post.caption: We’ll put you on the pill, that will sort you out. This is just your first period, wait until it settles. Ayla was only 11 years old when her symptoms started. Despite experiencing severe pain, it took 4 years of being dismissed before she finally received her endometriosis diagnosis. This is what endometriosis looks like in teens.  It’s not just a bad period, and it doesn’t just affect adults.

post.caption: We all have bad periods, you just have to get used to it. Ayla first started experiencing symptoms at 11 years old. It took years before anyone: took her seriously. This is what endo looks like in teenagers and why we need to do better

raw_metadata.catalog_title: We all have bad periods, you just have to get used to it. Ayla first ...

transcript.full_text: It was actually the day before I started high school. I got my period, so that was just completely not a good time at all. And it was actually the first day I was in PE. Normally I was doing track, I was doing dance and I'd done that since two years old, never had any issues. And then randomly on my first day we would do a PE and I fainted. I remember being in such debilitating pain. I got sent to school nurse at the time. I just remember looking down and I was bleeding everywhere and I just remember thinking like why am I soaking through pads and having to change my pads, you know, every ten minutes but at the time because it was the first day of my period I'd never ever experienced this before. I just thought oh you know maybe it's normal and you know I remember telling the teachers like oh like I need to get changed or I really don't feel well and they were like oh no you know you'll just get used to it and I was like but I'm in like severe pain and I felt like I couldn't really ask anyone else because they either hadn't gotten their period yet or if they had you know it wasn't any different to normal day life. But I remember thinking like paracetamol wasn't helping, I was passing out, I was dizzy, I was having to change my pad every ten minutes and you know going to school then you know like from primary school I was in every day, my attendance was always fine and then at high school it just dipped because at every time I'd get my period which was never regular either, it would be all over the place and I either would be sent home from school and be in the school nurse's office, I couldn't go to dance lessons and that was really unheard of from me. So that was when me myself and my family were like this isn't right so then that was when I went to the GP and kind of said I'm really unwell around my periods like nobody else seems to either be hospitalised or bedbound by their period like what's going on and I think the first few visits it was like oh you know you'll just get used to it you know it's because it's your first period you know and I just thought oh I'd go for medical help and everyone tells me you know it's fine that you'll get used to it and I definitely believe that for quite some time but it just got worse and worse and yeah.

post.caption: A negative MRI does NOT mean you're in the clear! MRI is powerful, especially for endometriosis affecting the bowel, appendix, or pouch of Douglas, but it doesn't always correlate with what's actually found during surgery. Sometimes a two-stage procedure is needed simply because imaging didn't capture the full picture. Your symptoms are valid even when your scan says otherwise.

raw_metadata.catalog_title: A negative MRI does NOT mean you're in the clear! MRI is powerful, es...

transcript.full_text: MRI is good in studies it comes with sort of a relatively equal comparison to transvaginal scan the benefit of transvaginal scan is it's cheaper and it's more accessible however it does depend on the skill of the person using it like with everything okay so even more so than MRI MRI is about the interpretation of the result transvaginal scan is about how you use the probe and then interpret the results that you get MRI is very effective in some hospitals it's what's available you know it that's people's practice and they will tend to have radiologists who are extremely efficient and expert analyzing MRI result MRI is great for assessing endometriosis affecting above the rectal sigmoid so that's up to the large bowel the cecum the appendix small bowel going upwards it can be very effective in the pouch of Douglas however in my experience I find that sometimes there is not a correlation between the laparoscopic findings and the MRI findings often the laparoscopic findings which is the gold standard is more indicates worse thing so that's one of the issues with MRI is sometimes it doesn't correlate with laparoscopic findings and when you're doing your procedure sometimes not always but sometimes you may have to do a two stage procedure because it hasn't given you enough information and in fact you do need to do either further investigations or further procedure with a colorectal surgeon

post.caption: @Laura | Women’s Health Creator struggled with her own endometriosis symptoms and found herself building a community around it on TikTok. Worth checking out 💛

transcript.full_text: The benefits of MRI depends on who's reading it. If you've got it, the radiologist needs to have an interest in it. post.caption: Endometriosis takes. It takes your time, your energy, and life as you knew it. But it doesn't have to be the end of the story. @Laura | Women’s Health Creator is living proof that struggle can be transformed into something powerful. She turied her experience with endometriosis into advocacy, content creation, and a nonprofit events, building a community that helps countless women navigate the same road she once walked alone. You are not alone and you don't have to stay that way. Check out @Your Endo Events - a London meetup is coming soon.

comments.text:
Completely agree AI can be so helpful!
I had a laparscopy in December and they found adhesions on my bowel and my ovary was adhered to my pelvic wall. No active endo but did find old pelvic scarring. They managed to release some adhesions on my bowel but couldn't do anything with my adheared ovary as it was risking my utera (I never know how to spell that 😂)
I went back for my follow up in March and told my consultant I was still in lots of pain. She completely dismissed my pain and told me that my adheared ovary wouldn't cause me that much pain (I'm struggling to walk as I have sciatic type pain in my leg and my ovary literally feels like it's pulsating and being ripped out of my body!) She just told me to loose weight and the pain should pass!
This didn't sit right with me so I used Chat GBT to deep dive into my issues. Through this, I was able to educate myself on a lot of things. It told me that I should be referred to an endo specialist as my laparscopy was incomplete, there was a risk to my utera (that word again!), even though she had released some adhesions on my bowel.. that should have been done by a specialist and that due to my ovary being adhered to my pelvic wall, it's classed as pelvic distortion. All of these fit the criteria to be referred to an endo specialist, which she should have done right after my surgery 🙄
I then asked Chat GBT to create a letter I can send to my GP requesting for a second opinion and to be seen by an endo specialist.
The letter was brilliant and really clear. I sent it off not really expecting much back but within a week my GP called me and couldn't believe I had been dismissed like that and agreed with me that I should be referred to an endo specialist! She sent the referral off to the Endo specialist centre on Oxford as that's the closest place to. me and my referral has been accepted!! All this because of AI!
I now have the long wait to be seen but I would rather wait it out to be seen by a specialist than a general gynocologist that thinks loosing weight is the only key to becoming pain free! (I'm 14 stone so not too overweight either!) So thank you AI!

post.caption: You asked, Liz answered! Can endometriosis be over or under-diagnosed on MRI? Our endo CNS breaks it down.

raw_metadata.catalog_title: You asked, Liz answered! Can endometriosis be over or under-diagnosed...

transcript.full_text: So, MRI, can I have overdiagnosis underdiagnosis andometrosis? Is this true? Well, I would say MRI is particularly effective for identifying endometrosis, which affects the bowel above the rectal sigmoid junction. Trans vaginal scan is far more effective in our practice at least in assessing everything else in the pelvis. MRI is the only available resource in many places though, for assessing for rectal vaginal endometrosis, and therefore depends on the quality of the assessment and the analysis of the information which is gained through the MRI, and that depends on user interpretation. But we're working on it, there's a lot of education going on, and it's rapidly improving.

transcript.full_text: And somebody has asked, was there a conclusion that laparoscopic was far less successful than a full open surgery as far the condition worsening versus being better treated down the road? And I've terrible eyesight. So you can't see it nearly as well. The other beauty of laparoscopic surgery is that you use your instruments to feel the tissue and you can actually feel where they have normal tissue is. transcript.full_text: Hi, good afternoon everybody. It's Lizzie Brewing here again. There's nice to all again. Thank you so many to all of you who have looked at our 360 degree video. It's been an incredible response. We are very very very happy that we are hopefully helping you. There have been some questions raised and I'm just going to address them. So the first question was what point do we ask for this? Well whenever you're talking about surgery, basically, you can just bring it up in the conversation when you're with your consultant or your medical professional and they're discussing with you about the options of care. They will discuss medical and they will discuss surgical. When they're talking about the surgery, they'll say, well, what do you look at? Which areas of my body would end up with the pelvis? Do you look at when you're doing the assessment? And that gives you your avenue into ask for all the things which we stated in the 360 degree. In other words, are you going to look at my diaphragm? Are you going to look at my appendix? Are you going to look at my bowel or on the top to the bottom? Are you going to check the pouch of Douglas? Are you going to look at both ovaries above and below? Are you going to look at the space between the uterus and the womb between the uterus and the pouch of Douglas? And when you're looking at the pouch of Douglas, which is the base of the pelvis, are you going to elevate that so that you can see the skin stretched out, so just to see if there's any often enemy tools that may hide particular to the general, will hide within that space? And that can be asked for very reasonably, it's not an unreasonable request on your behalf to know. Also, are you going to have pictures or video? Or both that I can then access and potentially have a copy for my own records afterwards? And I think that's a perfectly reasonable discussion to have with your medical professional.

post.caption: It's not all doom and gloom. Researchers are now describing endometriosis as a "cancer-mimicking" disease. Meaning it shares biological features with cancer in the way it grows, invades tissue, and resists treatment. This isn't just terminology. It's proof that people are actively working to change how this condition is understood and taken seriously. Liz is an Endometriosis Clinical Nurse Specialist based in Wales, offering online consultations. If you want to speak to her, link in bio.

There are ways to improve it. Unfortunately, it is a condition which may take a while to settle and may always be a problem, but there are ways of adapting so that you can have a good quality of life, maximise your quality of life and your ability to function. post.caption: Endometriosis isn't just about painful periods. Chronic fatigue is one of its most common symptoms, and one of the most dismissed, because fatigue can have so many causes, it's rarely the first thing that points doctors toward endometriosis. If you're exhausted in ways that feel bigger than your cycle, it's worth investigating. Liz is an Endometriosis Clinical Nurse Specialist who offers online consultations. Anyone needing help navigating this, she's your person. Link in bio 🔗

transcript.full_text: I would say lethargy. I did a study years ago. I was asked to do a presentation about my clinic at the BSG in Caulf and I did it and I thought it was boring. So I've texted 100 patients. Guess how many replied? With 10 questions. 100%. 100% replied. 100 women. 95% had fatigue. I gave them all the symptoms. What did they have? 70% had dysparonia, painful intercourse. 80% had painful periods. And that again is an issue because fatigue could be many things. It could be that you've been working too hard. It could be that you're not eating well, been dieting, you're drinking too much alcohol. You know it could be many many things and the fatigue we believe is due to like an ongoing inflammatory process in the body which is a low ticking. It's like a drip in your roof you know which is going to make your ceiling fall but you don't really know it's there until the ceiling falls down. That's what it is.

There are easy questions which will mark you towards endometriosis but you need to ask them and you need to listen to the answers and that's the important thing. But there are complex symptoms with endometriosis. You get pain on intercourse, you get pain opening your vials, pain opening your bladder but those also happen for other things as well and what happens unfortunately is that people get multiple referrals to many other people and they may even get referrals to gynecology but not get asked that question is this endometriosis. post.caption: 9 years. That's how long it takes, on average, to get an endometriosis diagnosis in the UK. And as much as the system needs to change, it's not the only reason. Awareness, language, being dismissed, not knowing what's "normal".  It all plays a part. Liz Bruen, Endometriosis Clinical Nurse Specialist with 20 years of experience, breaks down why the delay happens, and what you can actually do about it. Liz offers virtual consultations where she'll go through your full symptom history, help you understand your condition, and put together a care plan tailored to you. If you've been waiting, going in circles, or just need someone who actually gets it, her link is in bio.

transcript.full_text: So it's simple and complicated. Basically, the majority will start developing issues in their periods, okay? And unfortunately, there is a social and clinical belief that, oh, you have to get used to your periods. It's rubbish. Right? But that's education. Okay? So that's one of the issues. And then you've got all the other issues of being a teenager, can complicate things. And also, endometriosis can present as IBS because it gets bloating. The crucial factor that you need to know when you're going to see somebody is to say, this happens every month. But add that to the complex that we can have patients who have the increase of symptoms which happen every month. But then they will have significant pain for the rest of the month as well. And that's still ending. Because there's different types of endometriosis. It affects people differently. Nobody will present in the same way. It's really good to look at symptoms. Symptom tools, you know. So you just keep a record of your own tools and take that with you. The problem is that when you go to see your GP, you'll often have eight minutes or less to tell them what the problem is. If you're a teenager, you will find it difficult possibly to communicate it. If you're an adult and it's been going on for years, you're exasperated. So you just don't know, you know. So there's that break in communication, I feel.

post.caption: Periods can be painful, but if you need strong painkillers to be able to function? GET HELP! Painful periods are often one of the first and most well-known symptoms of endometriosis. But it doesn't stop there. It will progress. This is not just for those already diagnosed with endo, this is for parents, teachers and classmates too. Let's spread the awarnes about Endometriosis.  Liz Bruen was the first endometriosis nurse in Wales. She has now moved to a private virtual space, so if you want to book a consultation with her, go to the link in our bio.

raw_metadata.catalog_title: Periods can be painful, but if you need strong painkillers to be able...

transcript.full_text: If you're needing to use really strong pain killers, get help. Get help, 100%. If you're passing out on day one and you're in school, then your friends and your teachers should be noticing it and saying, this ain't normal. I never passed out on my period ever, but I know lots of people who do. So we need people going into schools and educating because this is not something you get at 18 and 21. This can start when you are 11 and 12 and it will impact on your education, your ability to socialize, your ability to make friends. It'll get you labeled and the girls don't want to be labeled. They don't want to be different to everybody else. So these are all the other factors which are brought in there as well.

post.caption: Responding to a question from today: Can someone explain why excision, not ablation? Short answer: Surface level isn't enough. Liz Bruen (Endometriosis CNS & Surgical Assistant) explains that ablation is just "skimming." Treating what’s on the surface is only half the battle.  If you want to understand why endometriosis symptoms often "re-surface" after surgery, listen to this.

raw_metadata.catalog_title: Responding to a question from today: Can someone explain why excision...

transcript.full_text: Excision, not ablation. Ablation only takes the surface. It's like skimming something. If you need, if your plaster's gone on the wall, skimming it is not going to make any difference is it, because it's still going to blow. Yeah? Same difference. Endometriosis is too deep to skim over the top of it. It goes too deep. Hi, my name is Liz Bruin. I am an Endometriosis Clinical Specialist. I was the very first Endometriosis nurse since 2006 in Wales.

post.caption: This is for anyone heading into laparoscopic surgery for endo, or those currently on the waiting list. We recently had the chance to chat with Liz Bruen, a private Endometriosis Clinical Nurse  Specialist in Wales. What she shared about the laparoscopy process was absolutely mind-blowing. If you would like to speak to Liz yourself, you can find prices, availability and book a zoom appointment with her directly by visiting the link in our bio!

raw_metadata.catalog_title: This is for anyone heading into laparoscopic surgery for endo, or tho...

transcript.full_text: If you have a laparoscopy of somebody who doesn't know what they're looking for, they'll miss it. You need to do what we call a 360 degree assessment, and I think everyone should ask for this. I tell every patient, wherever you're going to have surgery with, say, I want to have a 360 degree assessment, I want to have pictures to show that you've looked at my diaphragm, checked whether I've got thoracic endo, checked all my bowels, checked my appendix, checked my ovaries, checked my uterus to bladder, and then uterus to pouch of Douglas, checked the bowel, moved the bowel around, checked to see whether I've got any hiding, because if you have rect vaginal endo, which is endometriosis, sitting in the pouch of Douglas. So imagine, imagine that's the pouch of Douglas. You can see that. Okay, so imagine if I elevate that like that, okay, and I had a spot in there. You can't see it when it's like that. When I open it up like that, you can see it, and that's what people need to do. You need to elevate the pouch of Douglas, so that's the first thing, and then excision, not ablation.

post.caption: From a heartbreaking diagnosis to a community of 30k people (from this week!). But the numbers can be, to an extent, terrifying, as Amy has admitted. The sense of not being alone is what makes the endo community so strong and truly supportive. Essentially, the community around endo is what helps many to cope mentally. How did the endo community help you? @growwithendo

transcript.full_text: See, this is the good news of social media. Yeah, it's not all doom and gloom. You have a community of almost 30,000 people. How does that feel? Terrifying. Absolutely terrifying. No, I love it. It can be really scary. I'll be honest with you, like it really can. I love sharing my story and my opinions. But obviously, when you're on social media, not everyone would agree with you. People aren't afraid to say their true feelings because, you know, they can hide behind a screen. But I'm so lucky that the community that I've gone on Instagram are absolutely fantastic. Like I literally love every single one of them. I just wish I had the time to sit down and talk to them all individually. So as terrifying as it is, I absolutely love it.
